value.Title: Control System for Carbon Intensity Management in a Hydrogen Supply Network

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-M743-RSVX-H3T9-00000-00Control System for Carbon Intensity Management in a Hydrogen Supply Network2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:00ZLexisNexisControl System for Carbon Intensity Management in a Hydrogen Supply NetworkTargeted News ServiceAlexandria, Va.

ALEXANDRIA, Virginia, May 12 -- AIR PRODUCTS & CHEMICALS, INC., Allentown, Pennsylvania has been assigned a patent (No. US 12625475 B2, initially filed Aug. 18, 2023) developed by Pratik Misra, Breinigsville, Pennsylvania, and Sanjay Mehta, Orefield, Pennsylvania, for "Control system for carbon intensity management in a hydrogen supply network."

A computer-implemented method of providing hydrogen having a defined carbon intensity (CI) value to an end user location, the process comprising: selecting a total end-to-end maximum CI value for the hydrogen from production to delivery of the hydrogen to an end user location; receiving one or more feedstocks; receiving product CI values associated with each feedstock and/or the produced hydrogen; receiving demand data defining the end user demand for the hydrogen; receiving renewable power data; defining, in an optimization model, a plurality of constraints; generating, using the optimization model, a control strategy for control of the one or more industrial plants; and controlling the industrial plants in accordance with the values of the control variables to process the one or more feedstocks in order to provide a required quantity of hydrogen meeting the selected total end-to-end maximum CI value for use by an end user.

For more information about Targeted News Service federal patent awards please contact: Myron Struck, Editor, Direct: 703/866-4708, Cell: 703/304-1897, Myron@targetednews.com

value.Title: Coordinated Optimization Peak Shaving Method for Plurality of Power Supplies Based on Fluctuation Characteristics of Renewable Energy

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH7-55C3-RT9C-N3M3-00000-00Coordinated Optimization Peak Shaving Method for Plurality of Power Supplies Based on Fluctuation Characteristics of Renewable Energy2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisCoordinated Optimization Peak Shaving Method for Plurality of Power Supplies Based on Fluctuation Characteristics of Renewable EnergyTargeted News ServiceAlexandria, Va.

ALEXANDRIA, Virginia, May 5 -- STATE GRID CORPORATION OF CHINA, Beijing, China has been assigned a patent (No. US 12620807 B2, initially filed June 10, 2021) developed by seventeen inventors Qiang Zhou, Lanzhou, China; Yanqi Zhang, Lanzhou, China; Pengfei Gao, Lanzhou, China; Kun Ding, Lanzhou, China; Qingquan Lv, Lanzhou, China; Long Zhao, Lanzhou, China; Ruixiao Zhang, Lanzhou, China; Yanhong Ma, Lanzhou, China; Guodong Wu, Lanzhou, China; Chengjia Bao, Lanzhou, China; Jinping Zhang, Lanzhou, China; Dingmei Wang, Lanzhou, China; Jianmei Zhang, Lanzhou, China; Jin Li, Lanzhou, China; Zhenzhen Zhang, Lanzhou, China; Sheng Wang, Lanzhou, China; and Yongrui Zhang, Lanzhou, China, for "Coordinated optimization peak shaving method for plurality of power supplies based on fluctuation characteristics of renewable energy."

Disclosed is a coordinated peak shaving optimization method for a plurality of power supplies based on a fluctuation characteristic of a renewable energy source, including the following steps: s1: determining a weekly generated electricity quantity of hydropower based on an available capacity and a storage capacity of an electricity quantity; s2: predicting a renewable energy power generation curve and a load curve of a system weekly; s3: determining a start point of peak shaving of the hydropower based on an external transmission curve, the renewable energy generation curve, and the load curve of the system and a generating capacity of the hydropower; s4: determining a weekly peak shaving demand of the system; and s5: establishing an optimization model with a maximum peak shaving demand. The present disclosure proposes a reasonable arrangement for peak shaving, so as to resolve an accommodation problem caused by large-scale access of a renewable energy source.

For more information about Targeted News Service federal patent awards please contact: Myron Struck, Editor, Direct: 703/866-4708, Cell: 703/304-1897, Myron@targetednews.com

value.Title: Client Alert: Virginia Rooftop Solar Economics Change in Dominion Territory, Affecting Capital Planning and Project Design

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-M2G3-RW13-H33W-00000-00Client Alert: Virginia Rooftop Solar Economics Change in Dominion Territory, Affecting Capital Planning and Project Design2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisClient Alert: Virginia Rooftop Solar Economics Change in Dominion Territory, Affecting Capital Planning and Project Design

The views expressed in any and all content distributed by Newstex and its re-distributors (collectively, the "Newstex Authoritative Content") are solely those of the respective author(s) and not necessarily the views of Newstex or its re-distributors. Stories from such authors are provided "AS IS," with no warranties, and confer no rights. The material and information provided in Newstex Authoritative Content are for general information only and should not, in any respect, be relied on as professional advice. Newstex Authoritative Content is not "read and approved" before it is posted. Accordingly, neither Newstex nor its re-distributors make any claims, promises or guarantees about the accuracy, completeness, or adequacy of the information contained therein or linked to from such content, nor do they take responsibility for any aspect of such content. The Newstex Authoritative Content shall be construed as author-based content and commentary. Accordingly, no warranties or other guarantees are offered as to the quality of the opinions, commentary or anything else appearing in such Newstex Authoritative Content. Newstex and its re-distributors expressly reserve the right to delete stories at its and their sole discretion.

Whiteford Dale Mullen

May 7th, 2026 ( JD Supra - Delivered by Newstex )

Virginia's rooftop solar economics just changed in Dominion territory in ways that matter for capital planning and project design.

Last week, the State Corporation Commission (SCC) approved Dominion's 'NEM 2.0' tariff and set a new export credit rate of 5.829/kWh for customers who send more electricity back to the grid than they use over the annual netting period. That figure combines a 4.829/kWh avoided-cost rate with an additional 1/kWh to reflect avoided Renewable Portfolio Standard (RPS) compliance costs-explicit recognition that customer-owned solar reduces Dominion's need to procure renewable energy to meet statutory targets.

At the same time, the SCC left the core mechanics of Virginia net metering in place. Existing customers keep their current tariff. New customers will continue to offset their consumption at the retail rate first, carry forward excess as kilowatt-hour credits over 12 months and see the 5.829/kWh rate only on any true surplus at the end of the year. The Commission rejected Dominion's proposal to convert net metering into a 30-minute buy-and-sell construct with dollar-based credits and declined to add a new application fee, while allowing a $1 per month administrative charge for NEM 2.0 customers.

For businesses and homeowners evaluating solar-or operating solar-backed facilities-three effects are likely to be most immediate:

1. System sizing becomes more critical. Oversizing beyond a facility's load profile is less attractive financially when year-end surplus is compensated at 5.829/kWh rather than at a rate that roughly tracks full retail under the legacy structure.

2. The export credit is now formula-driven. By tying the rate to avoided energy and capacity costs plus a quantified RPS benefit, the SCC has created a clearer framework for valuing surplus energy and signaled that future adjustments will be grounded in cost-benefit analysis rather than wholesale structural change.

3. Value shifts toward integrated energy management. With tens of thousands of Virginia homes already using rooftop solar and large new loads, including data centers, coming onto the grid, the strongest economics will come from matching solar to on-site demand-potentially with storage, time-of-use rates and demand flexibility-rather than relying on export credits alone.

For Virginia companies, this turns rooftop and distributed solar from a straightforward equipment purchase into a regulatory and financial design exercise. The same array can produce very different returns depending on how it is structured under NEM 2.0, which tariffs apply, and how it is integrated with broader capital and energy strategies.

Link to the original story.

value.Title: Dominion solar credit rate to decline

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ8-68X3-SGDB-7124-00000-00Dominion solar credit rate to decline2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisNEWSADVANCE.COMDominion solar credit rate to declineStaff Writer

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order. The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

BLACKSBURG: The U.S. Department of Energy has selected Virginia Tech and the Southeastern Universities Research Association to manage and operate Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facility, a nuclear physics research facility in Newport News.

Virginia Tech's role marks the first time a university has been selected to help manage the facility in its 42-year history, according to an announcement made by the university last week.

Jefferson Lab is home to the Continuous Electron Beam Accelerator Facility, a particle accelerator that allows scientists to study the structure of protons and neutrons and better understand the behavior of the particles inside them. The laboratory employs about 840 people and operates with an annual budget of about $238 million, according to Jefferson Lab officials.

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order. value.Title: Dominion solar credit rate to decline

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ6-2T83-RYG7-909S-00000-00Dominion solar credit rate to decline2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisHERALDCOURIER.COMDominion solar credit rate to declineStaff Writer

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order. The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553-cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

BLACKSBURG: The U.S. Department of Energy has selected Virginia Tech and the Southeastern Universities Research Association to manage and operate Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facility, a nuclear physics research facility in Newport News.

Virginia Tech's role marks the first time a university has been selected to help manage the facility in its 42-year history, according to an announcement made by the university last week.

Jefferson Lab is home to the Continuous Electron Beam Accelerator Facility, a particle accelerator that allows scientists to study the structure of protons and neutrons and better understand the behavior of the particles inside them. The laboratory employs about 840 people and operates with an annual budget of about $238 million, according to Jefferson Lab officials.

value.Title: Dominion Energy solar credit rate to decline Dominion solar credit rate to decline

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ1-6XJ3-RRW1-J4GY-00000-00Dominion Energy solar credit rate to decline2026-05-09T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisAPg. 4ADominion Energy solar credit rate to declineDominion solar credit rate to declineDAVE RESS Richmond TimesDispatch

RICHMOND - The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order.

The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553-cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

Solar industry sources hailed the SCC order as a rejection of Dominion's proposal for net metering - the program through which it delivers power to people with solar panels and agrees to take power from those panels when they produce more electricity than the panel owner needs.

The key is language in the order that says Dominion needs to track the actual electricity net-metering that customers export rather than tally the money value of that flow, as Dominion proposed.

Doing that makes it easier for customers to take advantage of periods - sunny ones, for instance - when their panels produce more power than they need and use that surplus to offset times - in the winter, for instance - when they take more power from the grid than their panels produce, solar industry officials say.

"Dominion's proposal would have pulled the rug out from under thousands of Virginians who want to lower their bills and generate their own clean energy," said Shawn Kelly, a managing director for state regulatory issues at the trade association Advanced Energy United.

Solar advocates had campaigned to keep the 14-cent rate, but now say relatively few Dominion customers actually get it, because over the course of a year, most will end up receiving more power from the grid than they ship to it from their panels.

What most solar panel owners gain is that the amount of electricity they draw from Dominion is less than it would have been if they did not have panels.

That's a key selling point for the solar industry. Over time, generally years, those savings repay solar panel owners for the upfront cost of their installations.

"The State Corporation Commission was right to reject Dominion's request that would have made it much harder for Virginians to lower their electricity bills and contribute to grid reliability by investing in rooftop solar," said Kevin Lucas, vice president of policy analysis at the Solar Energy Industries Association.

"We've always known that the benefits of net metering extend far beyond any one home that has rooftop solar, and it is great to see regulators affirm the widespread benefits of rooftop solar in their ruling," he said.

Charles Gerena, pictured in 2023, had solar roof panels installed at his home after joining the Solar United Neighbors co-op. Courtesy Solar United Neighbors Charles Gerena, pictured in 2023, had solar roof panels installed at his home after joining the Solar United Neighbors co-op. Courtesy Solar United Neighbors

urn:contentItem:6JJ8-68X3-SGDB-71J8-00000-00Dominion solar credit rate to decline2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisRICHMOND.COMDominion solar credit rate to declineStaff Writer

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order. The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553-cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

BLACKSBURG: The U.S. Department of Energy has selected Virginia Tech and the Southeastern Universities Research Association to manage and operate Thomas Jefferson National Accelerator Facility, a nuclear physics research facility in Newport News.

Virginia Tech's role marks the first time a university has been selected to help manage the facility in its 42-year history, according to an announcement made by the university last week.

Jefferson Lab is home to the Continuous Electron Beam Accelerator Facility, a particle accelerator that allows scientists to study the structure of protons and neutrons and better understand the behavior of the particles inside them. The laboratory employs about 840 people and operates with an annual budget of about $238 million, according to Jefferson Lab officials.

value.Title: Dominion solar credit rate to decline

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9GF-H541-00000-00Dominion solar credit rate to decline2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisFREDERICKSBURG.COMDominion solar credit rate to declineDAVE RESS Richmond TimesDispatch

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order.

The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553-cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

What's behind Dominion's push to cut credit for solar panels?

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

Solar industry sources hailed the SCC order as a rejection of Dominion's proposal for net metering the program through which it delivers power to people with solar panels and agrees to take power from those panels when they produce more electricity than the panel owner needs.

The key is language in the order that says Dominion needs to track the actual electricity net-metering that customers export rather than tally the money value of that flow, as Dominion proposed.

Doing that makes it easier for customers to take advantage of periods sunny ones, for instance when their panels produce more power than they need and use that surplus to offset times in the winter, for instance when they take more power from the grid than their panels produce, solar industry officials say.

"Dominion's proposal would have pulled the rug out from under thousands of Virginians who want to lower their bills and generate their own clean energy," said Shawn Kelly, a managing director for state regulatory issues at the trade association Advanced Energy United.

Solar advocates had campaigned to keep the 14-cent rate, but now say relatively few Dominion customers actually get it, because over the course of a year, most will end up receiving more power from the grid than they ship to it from their panels.

What most solar panel owners gain is that the amount of electricity they draw from Dominion is less than it would have been if they did not have panels.

That's a key selling point for the solar industry. Over time, generally years, those savings repay solar panel owners for the upfront cost of their installations.

"The State Corporation Commission was right to reject Dominion's request that would have made it much harder for Virginians to lower their electricity bills and contribute to grid reliability by investing in rooftop solar," said Kevin Lucas, vice president of policy analysis at the Solar Energy Industries Association.

"We've always known that the benefits of net metering extend far beyond any one home that has rooftop solar, and it is great to see regulators affirm the widespread benefits of rooftop solar in their ruling," he said.

value.Title: Advanced Energy United : Virginia Regulators Defend Net Metering in Dominion Territory

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGJ-3333-SBBY-F0BT-00000-00Advanced Energy United: Virginia Regulators Defend Net Metering in Dominion Territory2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisAdvanced Energy United: Virginia Regulators Defend Net Metering in Dominion Territory

Key Highlights:

* Virginia State Corporation Commission rejects Dominion Energy's proposal to reduce net metering value.

* Decision preserves net energy metering policy, benefiting customers and local clean energy jobs.

* Net metering allows customers to receive fair credit for excess clean energy sent to the grid.

Original Press Release:

RICHMOND, Virginia, May 1 -- Advanced Energy United issued the following news release:

- preserves key rooftop-solar policy that helps families cut energy bills

The Virginia State Corporation Commission rejected a proposal from Dominion Energy that would have drastically reduced the value of customer-owned clean energy across its service territory.

"Dominion's proposal would have pulled the rug out from under thousands of Virginians who want to lower their bills and generate their own clean energy," said Shawn Kelly, Managing Director, State Regulatory at Advanced Energy United. "The Commission rejected that approach and upheld a policy that benefits customers, communities, and the grid."

Net energy metering allows customers with rooftop solar and other forms of clean energy to receive fair credit for the electricity they generate and send back to the grid, which reduces overall system costs and supports local clean energy jobs.

Key takeaways from the ruling:

* Commission's order denies Dominion's attempt to dramatically reduce net metering customer savings.

* Commission affirms that net metering provides the benefits of reduced reliance on carbon generation, enhance reliability and resiliency for the grid through increased transmission and distribution capacity, and fewer line losses.

* 6% statutory cap remains in place (Dominion is currently below 3%).

Advanced Energy United applauds the decision, which affirms the important role customer-owned clean energy plays in Virginia's future energy economy.

[Category: Renewable Energy, Energy, Regulatory and Policy Developments]

Source: Advanced Energy United

value.Title: Geo POWER Act Legislation by Rep. Begich Analyzed

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JG9-T4S3-RX1F-W0PT-00000-00Geo POWER Act Legislation by Rep. Begich Analyzed2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisGeo POWER Act Legislation by Rep. Begich AnalyzedBailey Malota, Targeted News Service

WASHINGTON, May 1 -- The Geo POWER Act, originally introduced by Rep. Nicholas J. Begich III, R-AK, on April 22, 2026, has been analyzed by the Congressional Research Service. This legislation aims to advance next-generation geothermal electricity generation by facilitating demonstration projects in new regions across the United States.

The primary motivation behind the Geo POWER Act is to enhance geothermal energy utilization, an underexplored renewable resource, to address both energy needs and climate concerns. By focusing on overcoming high upfront capital costs and developing robust data to minimize risks associated with geothermal projects, the legislation seeks to accelerate the commercialization of innovative geothermal technologies.

Key provisions of the Act include the establishment of a Milestone-Based Geothermal Demonstration Program. This program will utilize innovative financing to support projects in low-permeability and impermeable reservoirs, particularly in regions that currently lack geothermal electricity generation capabilities. The emphasis on diverse geologies aims to catalyze widespread adoption and investment in geothermal technologies.

The legislation also highlights the necessity of producing geothermal projects that can provide a minimum electricity generation capacity of 30 megawatts. By prioritizing projects that can attract substantial private sector investment, the Geo POWER Act intends to stimulate economic growth and job creation in the clean energy sector.

In addition, the Act calls for the collection and dissemination of data to inform future developments in geothermal resources and technologies. This approach is designed to encourage private investments while mitigating risks associated with new geothermal explorations.

As the energy landscape continues to evolve, the Geo POWER Act represents a critical step towards unlocking the potential of geothermal energy as a sustainable resource for America's future energy needs.

The bill (H.R. 8437) has 1 co-sponsor: Rep. Susie Lee, D-NV.

value.Title: Geothermal Ombudsman for National Deployment and Optimal Reviews Act Legislation by Sen. Cortez Masto Analyzed

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JG9-T4S3-RX1F-W0SW-00000-00Geothermal Ombudsman for National Deployment and Optimal Reviews Act Legislation by Sen. Cortez Masto Analyzed2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisGeothermal Ombudsman for National Deployment and Optimal Reviews Act Legislation by Sen. Cortez Masto AnalyzedBailey Malota, Targeted News Service

WASHINGTON, May 1 -- The Geothermal Ombudsman for National Deployment and Optimal Reviews Act, originally introduced by Sen. Catherine Cortez Masto, D-NV, on April 23, 2026, has been analyzed by the Congressional Research Service. This legislation aims to create a Geothermal Ombudsman and establish a Geothermal Permitting Task Force within the Bureau of Land Management to streamline the permitting process for geothermal energy projects on public lands.

Geothermal energy, hailed for its sustainability and reliability, is a crucial component of the United States' transition to renewable energy. However, the current permitting process can be cumbersome and slow, hindering the development of geothermal projects. This legislation seeks to address these challenges by appointing a Geothermal Ombudsman who will serve as a liaison among various Bureau of Land Management offices and facilitate better communication and efficiency in permit processing.

The Geothermal Ombudsman is tasked with dispute resolution, best practice development for geothermal authorizations, and monitoring permit processing timelines. Alongside the Ombudsman, the newly established Geothermal Permitting Task Force will support these initiatives, enhancing collaboration across the Bureau's various offices. This coordinated approach is expected to minimize delays in project approval and encourage the development of geothermal projects that harness the country's untapped renewable potential.

The establishment of this ombudsman position reflects a growing commitment to expand America's renewable energy portfolio. As the nation faces urgent challenges related to climate change and energy independence, optimizing permitting processes for geothermal energy can lead to significant advancements in clean energy deployment. Enhanced regulatory support through this legislation represents a strategic move to accelerate the growth of geothermal energy, making it a more viable option for meeting energy needs in the future.

The bill (S. 4383) has 1 co-sponsor: Sen. James E. Risch, R-ID.

* * # * *

value.Title: Skyview Ventures integrates monitoring program for beekeeping on solar project sites

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-0C53-RS65-K1KM-00000-00Skyview Ventures integrates monitoring program for beekeeping on solar project sites2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:01:49ZLexisNexisSkyview Ventures integrates monitoring program for beekeeping on solar project sites

Renewable energy investor Skyview Ventures is deploying an environmental monitoring program across its portfolio of solar projects to manage pollinator activity on these sites. Through its solar subsidiary, Davis Hill Development, Skyview is integrating HiveTracks biodiversity monitoring on eight solar projects in New York, West Virginia, Tennessee and Connecticut. Skyview invested in HiveTracks and tested the platform on some project sites, and plans to expand to the rest in 2026.

This partnership reflects our broader vision that renewable energy projects should be developed with long-term environmental stewardship in mind, said Andy Karetsky, CEO of Skyview Ventures. By integrating biodiversity monitoring, pollinator habitats and data-driven land management into our solar portfolio, we are demonstrating that solar projects can deliver environmental benefits beyond clean energy generation and play a meaningful role in supporting local ecosystems.

HiveTracks hires local beekeepers to work on these solar sites and supports them with proprietary monitoring. HiveTracks was developed to monitor ecosystem health and impacts of solar projects on the site over time, from pre-construction through operations all while producing honey. Honey produced on Skyview project sites in 2025 was donated to local food banks and given away as gifts.

With the data gathered on project sites regarding the health of pollinators, and plant diversity and abundance HiveTracks offers recommendations for proprietors to further improve local biodiversity.

Solar energy already provides environmental benefits, but with HiveTracks, we saw an opportunity to go further, said Maria Morales Ferrebus, solar development associate at Skyview Ventures. In our first year, the data provided valuable insights into ecosystem health across our sites. For example, we identified areas with low plant diversity, which can reduce resilience to drought conditions and increase erosion risk. By implementing HiveTracks recommendations, we can adapt our operations and maintenance practices to enhance plant diversity, improve soil health, and strengthen pollinator habitats.

Skyview stated in a press release that integrating HiveTrack has created a scalable modle supporting pollinator health and community agriculture, while generating guided data informing long-term land stewardship.

Solar sites represent a unique opportunity to support pollinators and monitor biodiversity at scale, said Max Rnzel, CEO of HiveTracks. Our partnership with Skyview Ventures demonstrates how beekeeping, environmental data, and renewable energy infrastructure can work together to create measurable environmental impact.

value.Title: Bonus Payments Legislation by Sen. Barrasso Analyzed

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK9-6YK3-RT3C-1007-00000-00Bonus Payments Legislation by Sen. Barrasso Analyzed2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisBonus Payments Legislation by Sen. Barrasso AnalyzedBailey Malota, Targeted News Service

WASHINGTON, May 14 -- The Bonus Payments, originally introduced by Sen. John Barrasso, R-WY, on April 28, 2026, has been analyzed by the Congressional Research Service. This legislation aims to amend the Mineral Leasing Act to facilitate the payment of bonus payments for certain coal leases, making them payable in ten equal annual installments.

The proposed changes to the Mineral Leasing Act underscore the increasing demand for coal leases, particularly in regions rich in natural resources. By allowing deferred bonus payments, Sen. Barrasso's bill offers a more manageable financial structure for entities interested in mining operations. This flexibility is expected to attract more companies to bid on coal leases, ultimately stimulating local economies and boosting employment in the resource extraction sector.

This legislative initiative comes in the context of broader discussions surrounding energy independence and economic growth. Although the coal industry has faced challenges in recent years due to a surge in renewable energy sources, many believe that traditional fossil fuels still play a crucial role in meeting the nation's energy demands. The bill seeks to ensure that the U.S. remains competitive in the global market for coal production by encouraging investment and participation in the leasing process.

In addition to economic benefits, the proposed installment payment system aims to provide a safety net for buyers, enabling them to better manage their financial obligations. By spreading out payments, the bill acknowledges the capital-intensive nature of mining operations and the financial unpredictability often faced by new ventures in the coal industry.

As the Senate considers this legislation, it illustrates a renewed focus on conventional energy sources, highlighting a complex balance between economic growth, energy needs, and environmental considerations. The bill's implications could reverberate throughout the coal industry and its associated labor markets.

The bill (S. 4410) has 3 co-sponsors: Sens. Cynthia M. Lummis, R-WY; Mike Lee, R-UT; Tim Sheehy, R-MT.

value.Title: May 08, 2026: Nanyang Technological University’s Zhao Yanli Inducted as AIMBE Fellow

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK3-R6T3-RSHC-749F-00000-00May 08, 2026: Nanyang Technological University’s Zhao Yanli Inducted as AIMBE Fellow2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 08, 2026: Nanyang Technological University’s Zhao Yanli Inducted as AIMBE Fellow

Nanyang Technological University (NTU Singapore) proudly announces that Professor Zhao Yanli from the School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology has been elected as a Fellow of the American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (AIMBE). The announcement comes following Professor Zhao's induction on the 13th of April, 2026, during the AIMBE Annual Event held in Arlington, Virginia, USA.

Professor Zhao Yanli has been recognized for his pioneering interdisciplinary research in materials chemistry and biomedical engineering. His work notably advances the fields of bioimaging, therapy, and innovative nanomedicine platforms, which has garnered him a prestigious position among the top two percent of medical and biological engineers. The College of Fellows is regarded as one of the highest professional distinctions in these fields.

At Nanyang Technological University, Professor Zhao leads a research group that focuses on developing integrated functional systems for targeted bioimaging and therapy in disease treatment. His team has made significant strides in creating therapeutic systems that not only target diseased cells but also enable imaging through optical methods while delivering therapeutic drugs precisely where needed. Their recent innovations include the development of single-metal atom biocatalysts intended for applications such as antitumor immunotherapy.

The single-metal atom biocatalysts designed by Professor Zhao's group are characterized by their high catalytic activity and selectivity, thanks to their well-defined structures. These systems have been custom-designed to suit the specific microenvironments of various diseases, showcasing their potential for efficient therapeutic outcomes. The research also delves into the atomic structure of these biocatalysts, optimizing their catalytic active sites and elucidating the mechanisms behind their biocatalytic processes.

The election of Professor Zhao Yanli to the AIMBE College of Fellows is a testament to the groundbreaking research taking place at Nanyang Technological University. His contributions not only elevate the profile of NTU in the realm of medical and biological engineering but also inspire future innovations in the field, further solidifying the university’s commitment to advancing science and technology.

INDEX

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

SECTION 2 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Nanyang Technological University (NTU), founded in 1991, is a public research university in Singapore. With over 7,000 employees and 33,000 students, it generates approximately $1.5 billion in annual revenue. Renowned for engineering, business, and sciences, NTU ranks among Asias top universities, excelling in AI, robotics, and sustainability research. Its campus hosts the Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine and cutting-edge facilities like the Smart Campus initiative. In 2024, NTU expanded its green energy research, focusing on hydrogen technologies. Competing with the National University of Singapore, NTUs mission is to drive innovation and global impact through interdisciplinary education and transformative research, fostering industry partnerships and societal contributions.

value.Title: NARUC : National Association of Clean Air Agencies Representative Mike Dowd Honored with the 2025-2026 National Council on Electricity Policy Brinch Award for Collaboration in Public Service

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JN0-TVC3-RVXP-N0FM-00000-00NARUC: National Association of Clean Air Agencies Representative Mike Dowd Honored with the 2025-2026 National Council on Electricity Policy Brinch Award for Collaboration in Public Service2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:22ZLexisNexisNARUC: National Association of Clean Air Agencies Representative Mike Dowd Honored with the 2025-2026 National Council on Electricity Policy Brinch Award for Collaboration in Public Service

Key Highlights:

* Award recognizes Dowd's dedication to connecting air regulators and energy decisionmakers.

* Dowd helped enhance communication and find common ground on complex topics.

* Nominated by National Association of Clean Air Agencies Executive Director Miles Keogh.

Original Press Release:

Washington, May 13 -- National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners issued the following news release:

At its annual meeting in Charleston, South Carolina, on May 5 and 6, 2026, the National Council on Electricity Policy presented the Jan Brinch Award for Collaboration in Public Service to Mike Dowd, former director of the Air and Renewable Energy Division for the Virginia Department of Environmental Quality.

Dowd's longstanding career in public service is marked by his dedication to connecting air regulators and energy decisionmakers, helping both sides to connect their silos, enhance communication and find common ground on complex topics. He was nominated for the award by National Association of Clean Air Agencies Executive Director Miles Keogh.

"[Dowd] pioneered many of Virginia's clean air and clean energy programs," said NCEP Vice Chair and Chair of the Regulatory Commission of Alaska John Espindola at the event. "He helped to mentor more than one generation of emerging leaders in this field, and each of them carries forward that spirit of working together."

Dowd exemplifies a true commitment to public service, having worked for more than 40 years in environmental law and policy. He joined the Virginia DEQ in 2003, acting as the agency's enforcement director, and was director of air from 2008 until he retired in December 2025. He worked previously as an attorney at a private practice in Washington, D.C. He co-chairs NACAA's Enforcement Committee and is a past NACAA board member.

"Reflecting on my years of public service, I've really had a blast - I can't think of any other career where I could have had as much positive impact and had as much fun. I would recommend a career in public service to anyone who is lucky enough to get the opportunity," said Dowd during his acceptance speech. "Another criterion to win this award is engagement and collaboration. [I]f you approach each collaborative effort with common sense, an open mind, follow the law and treat everyone with dignity, you'll probably have done a good job."

The Brinch Award was established by NCEP in 2019 to honor Jeannette "Jan" Brinch (ne Katz) and recognize public servants who, like her, see their roles as opportunities to unite people in pursuit of public interest goals within the electricity sector. Jan played a vital role in facilitating NCEP from 2014 to 2018. In her memory, her family created a dedication video in 2023. To see photos from this event, visit bit.ly/4tQY9yF.

To learn more about NCEP, visit www.naruc.org/ncep.

About NARUC

NARUC is a non-profit organization founded in 1889 whose members include the governmental agencies that are engaged in the regulation of utilities and carriers in the fifty states, the District of Columbia, Puerto Rico and the Virgin Islands. NARUC's member agencies regulate telecommunications, energy, and water utilities. NARUC represents the interests of state public utility commissions before the three branches of the federal government.

[Category: Electric Utilities, Award Wins, Climate Change]

Source: National Association of Regulatory Utility Commissioners

value.Title: Dominion proposes new power station

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-Y4R3-RSW6-J3V5-00000-00Dominion proposes new power station2026-05-12T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:49ZLexisNexisCUMBERLANDDominion proposes new power stationJeff Moore

Dominion Energy is proposing a 3,000-megawatt combined cycle natural gas power station in Cumberland County that company officials say would help meet rapidly growing electricity demand while bringing hundreds of jobs and significant tax revenue to the county.

The proposed Cumberland Energy Center would operate as a baseload power facility, running continuously to provide electricity to customers across Dominion Energy's service territory, according to Dominion Energy spokesman Jeremy Slayton and Director of Generation Development Brandon Martin during an interview Thursday.

Slayton said electricity demand across Dominion's service territory continues to increase at "historic levels."

"Our regional grid operator PJM forecasts that our demand will grow on average by about 5% annually over the next 20 years and more than doubling by 2045," Slayton said.

To meet that demand, Dominion plans to build 33 gigawatts of new generation capacity, according to its long-term planning documents filed with the Virginia State Corporation Commission.

"About 75% of this new generation would be carbon free," Slayton said, citing offshore wind, solar, battery storage and advanced nuclear technology. "The remaining 25% is natural gas."

The Cumberland Energy Center would fall into that natural gas category.

Slayton said the facility would operate "pretty much 24/7 providing that everyday power that our customers need."

Martin said the company's planning strategy requires a balance between renewable energy and what he described as "dispatchable generation," or power sources that can be turned on and off as needed regardless of weather conditions.

"It's not dependent on any weather events," Martin said. "What we're seeing is demand for power being more 24/7. So the solution also needs to be something that is also 24/7."

Martin explained that the proposed plant would use combined cycle technology, which is designed to improve efficiency by capturing waste heat generated during electricity production.

"What a combined cycle is, it has additional units on it to then be able to do that, but then take waste heat and create steam and be a more efficient process in the generation of that electricity," Martin said. "So it's a wonderful unit to be able to efficiently and affordably be able to provide that electricity."

The proposed facility would have a footprint of about 150 acres on a site where Dominion has about 900 acres under option, officials said. The property is located off Duncan Store Road.

Martin said the facility itself would occupy only a portion of the larger property and would be set well back from surrounding areas.

Slayton said natural features made the location attractive for the project.

"There are a lot of trees, wooded areas that can be the natural buffer for the project and kind of help shield some of the viewshed potential impacts," Slayton said.

"I've heard it described as almost kind of sitting in a bowl," he added.

Company officials also cited the presence of existing infrastructure near the site as a key factor in its selection.

"There is already constructed infrastructure, existing infrastructure nearby that we can take advantage of," Slayton said.

Martin said an existing transmission line runs along the property and likely would serve as the project's connection point to the regional electric grid, although PJM Interconnection still must complete its review process.

Officials said the company also intends to rely on existing natural gas pipeline infrastructure.

Slayton said Dominion is still finalizing arrangements regarding natural gas supply sources.

"There is a line that runs through there, but we're still having conversations about the gas and where we're going to procure it from," Slayton said.

Company officials repeatedly emphasized minimizing impacts on the surrounding community.

"Our goal is to utilize as much existing infrastructure that is already in the area to limit greenfield, meaning new construction that's not already there," Martin said.

Slayton acknowledged concerns residents often raise about projects of this scale, including noise, traffic, emissions and runoff, but said many details are still under development.

"As we continue along with the development of this facility, some of those items will come into clearer focus," Slayton said. "As they come into clearer focus, we will certainly share that information with the community."

He added that the process would remain "very open and transparent."

Dominion has not yet submitted a conditional use permit application for the project, Martin said, but the company has begun community outreach efforts and plans additional public meetings.

"We'll be continuing to work with the county on that process as well as having multiple open houses to be able to support a fulsome application and getting that community feedback and integrating that community feedback into that conditional use permit," Martin said.

The company also plans to seek an air permit through the Virginia Department of Environmental Quality and approval from the State Corporation Commission.

Slayton said two community meetings are now set, with both to be held at the Cartersville Volunteer Fire Department, 1600 Cartersville Rd.

Meetings will be held from 5:30 – 7 p.m., on both Thursday, May 28, and Monday, June 8, 2026.

A timeline provided by Dominion shows that planning and development on the project will continue through 2028, with work on key permitting and approvals during that period. Construction is projected to begin 2029, with completion expected in 2034.

ECONOMIC BENEFITS

Dominion representatives pointed to economic development benefits as a major component of the proposal.

Slayton said the project is expected to create about 450 construction jobs and "a little over 100 jobs" once operational, including both direct and indirect positions.

A project handout shows that for the state, the project will provide 1,362 direct and indirect regional jobs. It is expected to provide $295.5 million in regional economic activity ($71.8 million in Cumberland), with it totaling $696 million in statewide output.

He said the largest long-term benefit for Cumberland County would come through tax revenue.

"Over the life of the project, that's going to contribute more than $500 million in revenue to the county," Slayton said.

According to Slayton, Cumberland County's current general fund revenues total about $21 million annually. He said the Cumberland Energy Center is projected to generate roughly $28 million in tax revenue during its first year of operation.

"The first year of operation, the Cumberland Energy Center will more than double the property taxes that are coming into the county," Slayton said. "That is a huge windfall for the county."

He said Dominion also intends to work with local organizations and residents on additional community investments, although no specific programs have been announced.

"We're going to listen to the people who live there, and we're going to work with them to find out ways that we can continue giving back to the community beyond the jobs and tax revenue that this facility creates," he said.

The proposed Cumberland facility would not be Dominion's first combined cycle natural gas plant in Virginia.

Slayton pointed to the Brunswick County Power Station and Greensville County Power Station in Southside Virginia as examples of similar facilities that have operated since the mid-2010s.

"They've been operating efficiently, safely and have been regularly providing power to our customers," Slayton said. "There's nothing new about this technology."

Martin added that newer facilities continue to improve as technology evolves.

"It's like anytime technology evolves and gets better with time," Martin said.

Turtle Day 'Shellebration' set

The fourth annual Turtle Day "Shellebration" will be held at Twin Lakes State Park from 1 p.m. until 4 p.m., Saturday, May 23, outside of the Discovery Area.

The event honors World Turtle Day, an international observance dedicated to raising awareness about turtle conservation. World Turtle Day was established by American Tortoise Rescue, a nonprofit that operates a sanctuary to assist and rehome turtles and tortoises.

"Visitors can meet our local 'shellebrity,' Ranger Myrtle the turtle, and discover the important role she plays at the park," said Brianna Cram, chief ranger of visitor experience at Twin Lakes State Park. "Turtles are vital to the ecosystem, and Ranger Myrtle helps us share that message in a fun and engaging way."

This family friendly event will also include turtle-themed games, crafts, face painting and a photo station where you can put on a costume turtle shell and take a "shell-fie."

Merchandise with turtle themes will also be available for purchase from the Friends of Twin Lakes State Park, an all-volunteer nonprofit organization that supports the park through fundraising and volunteer efforts.

The Turtle Shellebration is free to attend, but the regular $7 parking fee does apply.

For information about upcoming events at Twin Lakes or other Virginia State Parks, visit virginiastateparks.gov.

value.Title: May 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJ7-CFP3-S020-J528-00000-00May 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS2026-05-10T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 09, 2026: TRACKING TRENDS: CONSIGLI CONSTRUCTION CO. & PEER INSIGHTS

On February 25, 2026, Consigli Construction Co. commenced work on the Williams College Museum of Art, marking the first purpose-built facility on the campus. Abigail Berkebile, a prominent figure at the company, noted the complex and unique design of the project, highlighting the thrill of witnessing the museum staff's enthusiasm as the building progresses. Berkebile also utilized the project's visibility to emphasize the diverse opportunities available for women in the industry, explaining that project teams rely heavily on expertise in accounting, IT, legal, marketing, and design, allowing women to contribute meaningfully without necessarily requiring a formal construction background. To maintain focus during the project, she highlighted a routine of organizing her calendar and setting aside time to connect with her team daily to foster collaboration.

In other industry developments, Suffolk Construction is making steady progress on a major interchange project on U.S. Route 58 near downtown Suffolk, Virginia. Effective May 5, 2026, the Virginia Department of Transportation began removing a long-term lane closure, allowing all three lanes of westbound traffic to flow freely and facilitating a new ramp and flyover for eastbound traffic. In the renewable energy space, Virgin Media O2 announced a 10-year Power Purchase Agreement on May 1, 2026, to source energy from Egg Power's 70MW solar farm in Suffolk, which is currently under construction and set to generate power by 2027. Mark Hardman, Director of Finance Operations at VMO2, stated, "This agreement with Egg Power is the latest step in Virgin Media O2's journey to achieve net zero emissions by the end of 2040. We're committed to growing and operating our business in a way that's good for people and the planet."

Meanwhile, Turner Construction Company achieved a significant milestone on April 28, 2026, with the topping out of the 72-megawatt Aligned IAD-06 Data Center in Frederick, Maryland. The 450,000-square-foot facility logged over 80,000 craft hours with approximately 345 skilled workers. Nick Sevier, Operations Manager at Turner, remarked, "Topping out this facility represents more than completing the structural frame. It reflects the strength of our partnership with Aligned, the dedication of our trade partners, and Turner's commitment to delivering this complex, high-performance data center safely and efficiently." On the same day, Turner also announced the start of vertical construction on the $900 million, 600,000-square-foot expansion of the Geisinger Wyoming Valley Medical Center in Pennsylvania, which will increase the hospital's capacity to 500 beds by the first quarter of 2028. David Kaminski, Vice President & Senior General Manager, noted, "This milestone marks an important step forward in delivering expanded, modern health care access for families across northeastern Pennsylvania."

Elsewhere in the sector, Gilbane Development announced on April 28, 2026, the appointment of Andreas Vlahakis as head of capital markets. Operating from New York City, Vlahakis will focus on refining funding strategies and executing ground-up multifamily and student housing investments, reporting directly to President and CEO James Patchett. Additionally, on April 3, 2026, Gilbane began leasing Phase II of its $135 million affordable housing redevelopment at Barnaby & 7th in Washington, D.C. The second phase includes 229 units across four buildings slated for completion by the summer of 2026, with twenty percent of the apartments designated as Permanent Supportive Housing managed by the district's Department of Homeless Services.

value.Title: Skyview Ventures Expands Biodiversity And Data-Driven Beekeeping Program Across Multi-State Solar Portfolio With Hivetracks

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-PDH3-RRM5-K2W8-00000-00Skyview Ventures Expands Biodiversity And Data-Driven Beekeeping Program Across Multi-State Solar Portfolio With Hivetracks2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:11:51ZLexisNexisSkyview Ventures Expands Biodiversity And Data-Driven Beekeeping Program Across Multi-State Solar Portfolio With Hivetracks

Innovative initiative integrates pollinator habitat, biodiversity monitoring, and environmental data collection across solar projects
NASHVILLE, Tenn. and CRESTON, N.C., May 5, 2026 /PRNewswire/ -- Skyview Ventures, a U.S.-based renewable energy investor, together with its solar company, Davis Hill Development, today announces the expansion of its partnership with HiveTracks, a biodiversity intelligence platform, to scale data-driven environmental monitoring and managed pollinator programs across its solar portfolio. The program integrates apiaries, environmental monitoring, and pollinator habitats into operating and developing solar projects across multiple U.S. states.


Continue Reading
Skyview Ventures is both an investor in HiveTracks and the company's first solar industry client. The collaboration began in 2025 with eight solar projects across New York, West Virginia, Tennessee, and Connecticut. Following the success of the initial rollout, Skyview Ventures plans to expand the program to additional sites in 2026.
"This partnership reflects our broader vision that renewable energy projects should be developed with long-term environmental stewardship in mind," said Andy Karetsky, CEO of Skyview Ventures. "By integrating biodiversity monitoring, pollinator habitats, and data-driven land management into our solar portfolio, we are demonstrating that solar projects can deliver environmental benefits beyond clean energy generation and play a meaningful role in supporting local ecosystems."
Each participating solar site is managed by local beekeepers recruited and supported by HiveTracks. These apiaries serve not only for honey production but also as environmental monitoring tools, offering insight into ecosystem health and land use impact over time
HiveTracks produces environmental metrics, visualized on a per site and portfolio level, generating actionable recommendations to improve ecological conditions and biodiversity. The data collected comprise pollinator and plant diversity and abundance, and are complemented with hive-based testing for eDNA and ecotoxicology. In 2025, the honey produced at the program's apiaries was bottled and donated to food banks and given away as gifts.
The program spans projects at multiple stages of development-from pre-construction through operations-allowing Skyview Ventures to evaluate environmental impact across the full lifecycle of solar project development. Following annual analysis, HiveTracks provides site-specific recommendations that allow Davis Hill Development, the project operator, to implement pollinator-friendly operations and maintenance practices, and improve habitat conditions over time.
"Solar energy already provides environmental benefits, but with HiveTracks, we saw an opportunity to go further," said Maria Morales Ferrebus, Solar Development Associate at Skyview Ventures. "In our first year, the data provided valuable insights into ecosystem health across our sites. For example, we identified areas with low plant diversity, which can reduce resilience to drought conditions and increase erosion risk. By implementing HiveTracks' recommendations, we can adapt our operations and maintenance practices to enhance plant diversity, improve soil health, and strengthen pollinator habitats."
Together, these site-level insights are informing how Skyview Ventures approaches land stewardship across its portfolio. By integrating apiaries and environmental monitoring into its solar projects, the company is creating a scalable model that supports pollinator health, enables community agriculture, and generates consistent, site-specific data to guide long-term land management decisions.
"Solar sites represent a unique opportunity to support pollinators and monitor biodiversity at scale," said Max Rünzel, CEO of HiveTracks. "Our partnership with Skyview Ventures demonstrates how beekeeping, environmental data, and renewable energy infrastructure can work together to create measurable environmental impact."
The expanded program reflects a broader trend in the renewable energy industry toward multi-use land strategies, where solar development is combined with agriculture, habitat restoration, and biodiversity initiatives.
About Skyview Ventures
Skyview Ventures has three operating businesses: Skyview Finance Company, one of the largest merchants of Environmental Attributes; Davis Hill Development, which develops, owns, and operates solar and battery storage facilities; and Skycharger, which develops, owns, and operates electric vehicle charging infrastructure. In addition, Skyview Ventures has over 30 venture investments on their portfolio.
The company owns 70 MW of solar assets across 15 states and over 600 EV charging stations. Headquartered in Nashville, TN, with additional offices in Greenwich, CT and Sacramento, CA, Skyview Ventures is a national leader in clean energy.
To learn more, visit .
About HiveTracks
HiveTracks is building the data infrastructure for biodiversity, enabling landowners, developers, and farmers to measure and improve ecological outcomes directly on-site. HiveTracks brings environmental monitoring and sustainable agriculture to the green energy sector by leveraging beehives as biosensors and empowering beekeepers as field ecologists. To learn more, visit .
About Davis Hill Development
Davis Hill Development (DHD), a subsidiary of Skyview Ventures, is a leading developer and owner of distributed solar projects with more than 180 operating assets across 15 states. Founded in 2013, DHD delivers turnkey clean energy solutions for businesses, municipalities, and landowners, with a growing pipeline of community and commercial solar projects. The company specializes in structuring projects that generate long-term value-reducing energy costs, providing lease revenue, and supporting energy equity through community solar access for low-to-moderate income (LMI) residents. Through its parent company, DHD also offers REC and carbon credit solutions to help organizations meet sustainability goals. Learn more at

Alexandra Pony

value.Title: Is Wall Street Bullish or Bearish on AES Corporation Stock?

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-WS63-RSR3-40VM-00000-00Is Wall Street Bullish or Bearish on AES Corporation Stock?2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:12:53ZLexisNexisEQUITY MARKETS NEWSIs Wall Street Bullish or Bearish on AES Corporation Stock?Kritika Sarmah

The AES Corporation (AES) is a prominent power generation and energy infrastructure company headquartered in Arlington, Virginia. Founded in 1981, AES is currently valued at $10.2 billion by market cap, and it focuses on producing and distributing electricity while increasingly pivoting toward renewable energy and energy storage solutions.

The utilities major has surpassed the broader market over the past year. AES stock has gained 43.5% over the past 52 weeks, compared to the S&P 500 Index's ($SPX) 29% surge. However, in 2026, the stock is down marginally, lagging behind the SPX's 5.6% rally.

More Top Stocks Daily:

Go behind Wall Street's hottest headlines with Barchart's Active Investor newsletter.

 

Looking closer, the stock has also outperformed the sector-focused State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund's (XLU) 17.7% surge over the past 52 weeks.

On Apr. 1, AES shares rose marginally after the company secured the required investor approvals to amend the terms of its 5.450% Senior Notes due 2028. As part of the consent solicitation, eligible bondholders will receive a consent fee of about $4.90 per $1,000 principal, totaling $2.25 million, for approving the proposed changes.

For fiscal 2026, ending in December, analysts expect AES to report an EPS of $2.29, representing a 2.1% year-over-year decline. The company has a mixed earnings surprise history. While it missed the Street's bottom-line estimates twice over the past four quarters, it has surpassed the projections on two other occasions.

The stock currently carries a consensus "Hold" rating, with all nine analysts maintaining neutral stances. This marks a clear shift in sentiment from two months ago, when AES Corporation held a more bullish "Moderate Buy" rating, suggesting a cooling in analyst confidence.

On Apr. 9, Susquehanna Financial Group downgraded AES Corporation to "Neutral" from "Positive" and lowered its price target to $15 from $16 ahead of Q1 earnings. The move reflects a more cautious outlook on the alternative energy sector, where rising power demand remains a key tailwind, but is increasingly offset by headwinds such as the removal of the 25D residential tax credit, stricter FEOC regulations, permitting challenges, and tariff pressures.

On the date of publication, Kritika Sarmah did not have (either directly or indirectly) positions in any of the securities mentioned in this article. All information and data in this article is solely for informational purposes. For more information please view the Barchart Disclosure Policy here.

value.Title: April 28, 2026: Mars Secures Major Clean Energy Pact in Lithuania

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGJ-RGS3-RS9T-F35N-00000-00April 28, 2026: Mars Secures Major Clean Energy Pact in Lithuania2026-05-02T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSApril 28, 2026: Mars Secures Major Clean Energy Pact in Lithuania

On April 28, 2026, Mars, Incorporated announced a long-term power purchase agreement (PPA) for the majority output from the Skuodas Wind Farm in Lithuania. The deal is expected to generate about 490 GWh of renewable electricity annually and reduce carbon emissions by approximately 120,000 tons per year. The Skuodas Wind Farm has an installed capacity of 158.4 MW and will supply renewable energy to Mars' consumption and value chain, supporting the Renewables Acceleration Program and the company's net-zero ambitions.

Kevin Rabinovitch, Global VP of Sustainability at Mars, called the arrangement a milestone for real-world climate action, noting that "this agreement with European Energy helps bring new wind power online in Lithuania and strengthens our ability to extend credible renewable electricity across our value chain." The project is scheduled to go live in 2028 and will reinforce Mars' Lithuania manufacturing facility with a reliable source of renewable electricity, while also expanding domestic wind capacity in Europe through the corporate PPA.

INDEX

SECTION 1 MARS, INCORPORATED PROFILE

SECTION 2 MARS, INCORPORATED TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 3 MARS, INCORPORATED RANKING

SECTION 1 MARS, INCORPORATED PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Mars, Incorporated, founded in 1911, is a family-owned global confectionery and pet care company headquartered in McLean, Virginia. Employing over 140,000 people across 80 countries, it generates over $45 billion annually. Known for brands like M&Ms, Snickers, and Pedigree, Mars operates in confectionery, pet care, and food. In 2024, it acquired Kellanova for $35.9 billion, expanding its snack portfolio. Its culture, guided by Five Principles (Quality, Responsibility, Mutuality, Efficiency, Freedom), emphasizes sustainability and employee well-being. Competing with Nestl, Mars stands out for its private ownership and sustainability goals, targeting net-zero emissions by 2050. Recent initiatives include investments in plant-based foods and digital supply chain solutions.

value.Title: Dominion solar credit rate set to decline Dominion solar credit rate to decline

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-GYN3-SGH8-00W6-00000-00Dominion solar credit rate set to decline2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisAPg. 2ADominion solar credit rate set to declineDominion solar credit rate to declineDAVE RESS Richmond TimesDispatch

The price Virginians with solar panels get for shipping power to Dominion Energy will decline, under a new State Corporation Commission order.

The commission approved an export credit rate of 5.829 cents per kilowatt hour for Dominion Energy customers who deliver more electricity from their solar panels than they take from Dominion's system.

That's more than the revised 4.829 cents rate Dominion had proposed but less than the current 14-cent credit rate. Dominion originally proposed a 9.553-cent rate but revised it to reflect the impact of a separate renewable energy program.

The Solar Energy Industries Association says more than 64,000 Virginia homes have installed rooftop solar panels.

Solar industry sources hailed the SCC order as a rejection of Dominion's proposal for net metering - the program through which it delivers power to people with solar panels and agrees to take power from those panels when they produce more electricity than the panel owner needs.

The key is language in the order that says Dominion needs to track the actual electricity net-metering that customers export rather than tally the money value of that flow, as Dominion proposed.

Doing that makes it easier for customers to take advantage of periods - sunny ones, for instance - when their panels produce more power than they need and use that surplus to offset times - in the winter, for instance - when they take more power from the grid than their panels produce, solar industry officials say.

"Dominion's proposal would have pulled the rug out from under thousands of Virginians who want to lower their bills and generate their own clean energy," said Shawn Kelly, a managing director for state regulatory issues at the trade association Advanced Energy United.

Solar advocates had campaigned to keep the 14-cent rate, but now say relatively few Dominion customers actually get it, because over the course of a year, most will end up receiving more power from the grid than they ship to it from their panels.

What most solar panel owners gain is that the amount of electricity they draw from Dominion is less than it would have been if they did not have panels.

That's a key selling point for the solar industry. Over time, generally years, those savings repay solar panel owners for the upfront cost of their installations.

"The State Corporation Commission was right to reject Dominion's request that would have made it much harder for Virginians to lower their electricity bills and contribute to grid reliability by investing in rooftop solar," said Kevin Lucas, vice president of policy analysis at the Solar Energy Industries Association.

"We've always known that the benefits of net metering extend far beyond any one home that has rooftop solar, and it is great to see regulators affirm the widespread benefits of rooftop solar in their ruling," he said.

Dave Ress (804) 649-6948dress@timesdispatch.com

value.Title: May 08, 2026: NTU Professor Zhao Yanli Named AIMBE Fellow for Pioneering Work in Materials Chemistry and Biomedical Engineering

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-7343-RSHD-P2TT-00000-00May 08, 2026: NTU Professor Zhao Yanli Named AIMBE Fellow for Pioneering Work in Materials Chemistry and Biomedical Engineering2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 08, 2026: NTU Professor Zhao Yanli Named AIMBE Fellow for Pioneering Work in Materials Chemistry and Biomedical Engineering

Nanyang Technological University (NTU Singapore) announced that Professor Zhao Yanli from the School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology has been elected as a Fellow of the American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (AIMBE). The induction occurred on April 13, 2026, during the AIMBE Annual Event in Arlington, Virginia, USA.

Zhao is recognized for interdisciplinary research at the intersection of materials chemistry and biomedical engineering, advancing bioimaging, therapy and nanomedicine platforms. He ranks among the top two percent of medical and biological engineers, and the College of Fellows is regarded as one of the most prestigious professional distinctions in the field. At NTU, Zhao leads a research group focused on integrating functional systems for targeted bioimaging and disease therapy. The team has been advancing therapeutic platforms that both image diseased tissue and deliver drugs precisely where needed, including the development of single-metal atom biocatalysts for antitumor immunotherapy. These biocatalysts exhibit high catalytic activity and selectivity due to well defined structures and are designed to suit the microenvironments of different diseases. The work also explores the atomic structure of the catalysts, optimizing active sites and elucidating their biocatalytic mechanisms.

INDEX

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

SECTION 2 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Nanyang Technological University (NTU), founded in 1991, is a public research university in Singapore. With over 7,000 employees and 33,000 students, it generates approximately $1.5 billion in annual revenue. Renowned for engineering, business, and sciences, NTU ranks among Asias top universities, excelling in AI, robotics, and sustainability research. Its campus hosts the Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine and cutting-edge facilities like the Smart Campus initiative. In 2024, NTU expanded its green energy research, focusing on hydrogen technologies. Competing with the National University of Singapore, NTUs mission is to drive innovation and global impact through interdisciplinary education and transformative research, fostering industry partnerships and societal contributions.

urn:contentItem:6JK3-D173-RWWB-R4JC-00000-00May 08, 2026: NTU Scientist Zhao Yanli Inducted into AIMBE College of Fellows, Highlighting Leadership in Medical Engineering2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 08, 2026: NTU Scientist Zhao Yanli Inducted into AIMBE College of Fellows, Highlighting Leadership in Medical Engineering

Nanyang Technological University (NTU Singapore) announced that Professor Zhao Yanli of the School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology has been elected as a Fellow of the American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (AIMBE). Zhao was inducted on April 13, 2026, at the AIMBE Annual Event in Arlington, Virginia, USA.

The honor recognizes Zhao for his interdisciplinary work at the interface of materials chemistry and biomedical engineering, including advances in bioimaging, therapy and nanomedicine platforms. He is among the top two percent of medical and biological engineers, and the AIMBE College of Fellows is widely regarded as one of the profession’s most prestigious distinctions.

At NTU Zhao leads a group focused on integrated functional systems for targeted bioimaging and therapy. The team has been advancing approaches that image diseased tissue while delivering drugs precisely where needed, and they are pursuing single-metal atom biocatalysts for antitumor immunotherapy. These catalysts show high catalytic activity and selectivity thanks to well defined structures and are tailor-made for different disease microenvironments.

Researchers continue to investigate the atomic structure of these biocatalysts, optimizing active sites and clarifying the mechanisms behind their biocatalytic actions. The election underscores NTU leadership in medical and biological engineering and reinforces NTU commitment to advancing science and technology.

value.Title: May 08, 2026: NTU Chemist Zhao Yanli Earns AIMBE Fellowship for Advances in Bioimaging and Nanomedicine

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK7-JTR3-RSBB-S029-00000-00May 08, 2026: NTU Chemist Zhao Yanli Earns AIMBE Fellowship for Advances in Bioimaging and Nanomedicine2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:16:48ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 08, 2026: NTU Chemist Zhao Yanli Earns AIMBE Fellowship for Advances in Bioimaging and Nanomedicine

NTU Singapore today announced that Professor Zhao Yanli of the School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology has been elected a Fellow of the American Institute for Medical and Biological Engineering (AIMBE). The induction took place on April 13, 2026, at the AIMBE Annual Event in Arlington, Virginia, USA.

Zhao is recognized for pioneering interdisciplinary work in materials chemistry and biomedical engineering that advances bioimaging, therapy and nanomedicine platforms. His contributions place him among the top two percent of medical and biological engineers, and the AIMBE College of Fellows is regarded as one of the highest professional distinctions in these fields.

At NTU, Zhao leads a research group focused on developing integrated functional systems for targeted bioimaging and therapy in disease treatment. The team is pursuing approaches that combine imaging capabilities with therapeutic delivery, enabling clinicians to visualize diseased tissue while administering drugs.

The group has recently developed single-metal atom biocatalysts designed for applications such as antitumor immunotherapy. These catalysts are characterized by high catalytic activity and selectivity, aided by well defined structures and tailored designs for the microenvironments of different diseases.

Researchers are studying the atomic structure of these biocatalysts to optimize catalytic active sites and to better understand the mechanisms behind their biocatalytic processes. Zhao says the election underscores the groundbreaking work taking place at NTU and reinforces the university commitment to advancing science and technology and to inspiring future innovations.

INDEX

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

SECTION 2 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 NANYANG TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

Nanyang Technological University (NTU), founded in 1991, is a public research university in Singapore. With over 7,000 employees and 33,000 students, it generates approximately $1.5 billion in annual revenue. Renowned for engineering, business, and sciences, NTU ranks among Asias top universities, excelling in AI, robotics, and sustainability research. Its campus hosts the Lee Kong Chian School of Medicine and cutting-edge facilities like the Smart Campus initiative. In 2024, NTU expanded its green energy research, focusing on hydrogen technologies. Competing with the National University of Singapore, NTUs mission is to drive innovation and global impact through interdisciplinary education and transformative research, fostering industry partnerships and societal contributions.
